title: Mark 9v33-37.
scripture_text: ‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?”He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them And he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven And whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me “If anyone causes one of these little ones—those who believe in me—to stumble, it would be better for them to have a large millstone hung around their neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to stumble! Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.’’
devotional:
‘’They came to Capernaum. When he was in the house, he asked them “What were you arguing about on the road?” But they kept quiet because on the way they had argued about who was the greatest. Sitting down, Jesus called the Twelve and said “Anyone who wants to be first must be the very last, and the servant of all.” He took a little child whom he placed among them. Taking the child in his arms, he said to them, ‘’Whoever welcomes one of these little children in my name welcomes me; and whoever welcomes me does not welcome me but the one who sent me.”

A visible lesson.

To help them understand true greatness; Jesus gave his disciples two examples of greatness.

The Servant.

Who is the servant that Jesus is talking about? The truly great person is a diakonos a deacon a servant, a person who spends his/her day taking care of other people. 'Diakonos' refers to one who runs errands and is synonymous with 'attendant' or 'waiter.' Look carefully at what Jesus says here, 'the servant of all.' Think about that, there is nobody unworthy of being served by us.

This is mind blowing. Diakonos is a humble role, and sometime we wonder whether Jesus is serious when he says that the servant is great, but we have seen evidence that he is. Servant-people like Mother Theresa, inspire great affection and wield great influence. They not only relieve the suffering of those whom they touch directly, but they also inspire greatness in people whom they have never met. Their witness draws people to Christ and Christian service.

But what kind of person would struggle with taking on the role of a servant? Someone, who is insecure, someone whose focus is getting his or her own needs met, someone who is not free in the inside. Our inability to express the attitude of a servant indicates that we don't really understand what it means to be a follower of Jesus. Having the heart of a servant is not a luxury that can be done without, but an essential requirement for life.

The Child

Matthew 18:2-3 tells 'He called a little child and had him stand among them. And he said. ‘Unless you change and become like little children you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore whoever humbles himself like this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven.' Many people read these words and jump to wrong conclusions. Their thinking is something like. 'We need to be as innocent as little children if we are going to be great in the kingdom of heaven.' This understanding is wrong.

The Jewish Scriptures do not support the argument that children were perceived as being innocent. Psalm 51:5 says 'Surely I was sinful at birth, sinful from the time my mother conceived me.' The Rabbi's taught that a person could sin even before they were born. On one occasion the disciples asked Jesus 'Rabbi who sinned this man or his parents that he was born blind? They taught that a baby could sin in the womb.

Once innocence is lost, it is lost, and to ask a sinful grown man to become innocent again is asking the impossible.

So in what way do we need to become like little children?

In the first-century Mediterranean culture, children were treated as property; they were possessions belonging to their fathers. 'Parents are right to ignore the child's point of view and to disregard the child's feelings.' (Malina, 55). 'Children owe their lives to the parents, a debt that they can never adequately repay.' (153). Children had no power, no privilege, and no prosperity. They lived only through the good will and care provided to them by others. The New Interpreter's Bible said that the child in this society was a 'non-person' and was 'socially invisible' and, it would be unusual for a child to be regularly in the company of Jesus and his disciples (637)

So when Jesus says to us ‘to become like little children’ he is saying 'l want you to be vulnerable and dependent so you can receive love.' Little children have this natural ability to receive love from others, and give love to others. In Marks account we are told that Jesus took the child in his arms. Intimacy with Jesus is only possible if we are open or humble enough to receive love.

But why do we as adults find it so difficult to receive love? Perhaps it’s because of our lack of humility. The Greek word Matthew uses in 18:3 for humble is tapeinoo, which in it's strongest form, means 'to humiliate.' If children were full of pride and weren't willing to humiliate themselves they'd never learn to walk or talk. What makes a child get up and try again after they've had a clumsy fall, is it not the love and encouragement of others? So it is in the Christian life. Humility of heart makes us sensitive and open to the love of God, and that is true greatness.
prayer: Father may I have a childlike capacity to receive your love.
html:
Mark 9v33-37.

Study Text: Matthew 18v1-9.
‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?”He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them And he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven And whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me “If anyone causes one of these little ones—those who believe in me—to stumble, it would be better for them to have a large millstone hung around their neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to stumble! Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.’’

‘’They came to Capernaum. When he was in the house, he asked them “What were you arguing about on the road?” But they kept quiet because on the way they had argued about who was the greatest. Sitting down, Jesus called the Twelve and said “Anyone who wants to be first must be the very last, and the servant of all.” He took a little child whom he placed among them. Taking the child in his arms, he said to them, ‘’Whoever welcomes one of these little children in my name welcomes me; and whoever welcomes me does not welcome me but the one who sent me.”

'Parents are right to ignore the child's point of view and to disregard the child's feelings.' (Malina, 55). 'Children owe their lives to the parents, a debt that they can never adequately repay.' (153). Children had no power, no privilege, and no prosperity. They lived only through the good will and care provided to them by others. The New Interpreter's Bible said that the child in this society was a 'non-person' and was 'socially invisible' and, it would be unusual for a child to be regularly in the company of Jesus and his disciples (637)

What makes a child get up and try again after they've had a clumsy fall, is it not the love and encouragement of others? So it is in the Christian life. Humility of heart makes us sensitive and open to the love of God, and that is true greatness.

Prayer: Father may I have a childlike capacity to receive your love.
